access.accessMethod: Read the checked-out CME submodule path
access.entityLabel: General Corpus source XML for PPlLan

entities.description: The XML artifact consumed by this repository's conversion workflow, supplied through the Kyvero-Press/CME git submodule.
entities.label: General Corpus source XML for PPlLan
notes: The source submodule is a separate repository and is not modified by this manifest.

rights.basis: At the pinned CME revision, the root LICENSE is the Unlicense and explicitly speaks of software. No file-specific notice was found establishing that its public-domain dedication applies to inherited textual data or markup, while the XML preserves OTA personal-scholarly-use language and derives from a 1978 critical edition.
rights.component: The checked-in PPlLan XML artifact and inherited electronic markup
rights.jurisdiction: Repository terms, source-provider terms, and intended place of reuse
notes: The repository license is recorded rather than ignored, but its software wording is not broadened to third-party text, editorial contribution, or provider markup without an explicit scope statement.
The Unlicense does not transfer to Schmidt's edition, Langland's underlying work, or Trinity's photographs.

access.accessMethod: U-M persistent name resolver to the digital item
notes: The resolver remains the exact legacy route, but its current Quod destination blocked automated inspection with an HTTP 403 challenge. That check does not prove that human access is unavailable.

access.accessMethod: Current Oxford Text Archive item page
notes: The item page is public. Its replacement pointer to accession 3261 resolved to a current 404 and is retained as a failed legacy route rather than a verified successor.

access.accessMethod: Exact DSpace bitstream routes advertised by the item record
notes: Anonymous GET requests redirected to the item page instead of delivering bytes; no file was cached. The review did not bypass authentication or infer that a login would necessarily grant access.

entities.description: A machine-readable Middle English B-text prepared from Schmidt's 1978 edition and historically distributed through the Oxford Text Archive and University of Michigan.
entities.label: U-M/OTA PPlLan electronic text
notes: The XML header records both U-M and OTA availability statements but does not identify the individual encoder or keying workflow.
The current OTA item points to replacement accession 3261, whose purl resolved to a 404 during this review.

rights.basis: The legacy header limits OTA use to personal scholarly purposes, while the current OTA item carries an Academic Use label and the current OTA license permits noncommercial research and teaching subject to its conditions. No item-specific open license was verified.
rights.component: The U-M/OTA machine-readable text, transcription choices, and electronic markup
rights.jurisdiction: Provider terms and intended place of reuse
notes: Public catalog access and academic-use permission are not equivalent to unrestricted commercial redistribution.
The current bitstreams were not anonymously retrievable, so their embedded terms could not be compared directly with the repository XML.

access.accessMethod: WorldCat bibliographic record and holdings discovery
access.entityLabel: A. V. C. Schmidt's 1978 critical edition of the B text
access.format: Catalog records for the print book
notes: These are bibliographic and holdings routes, not an open full-text copy of the exact edition.

bibliographic.citation: William Langland, The Vision of Piers Plowman: A Critical Edition of the B-Text Based on Trinity College Cambridge MS B.15.17 with Selected Variant Readings, ed. A. V. C. Schmidt (London: J. M. Dent; New York: E. P. Dutton, 1978).
bibliographic.publisher: J. M. Dent; E. P. Dutton
bibliographic.title: The Vision of Piers Plowman

entities.label: A. V. C. Schmidt's 1978 critical edition of the B text
notes: The XML header gives xlviii, 364 pages; public catalogs vary slightly over preliminary and total pagination, so the discrepancy is retained rather than silently normalized.
The edition is explicitly critical and W-based, with selected variants. A full copy was not anonymously inspectable, so additional witness sigla and supplied readings remain unresolved.

rights.basis: The edition was published in 1978 and no public-domain dedication or open license for its original editorial contribution was verified.
rights.component: Schmidt's 1978 edited readings, introduction, glosses, commentary, apparatus, and other original editorial contributions
rights.jurisdiction: United States and other applicable jurisdictions
notes: This does not place Langland's underlying medieval work under copyright.

access.accessMethod: Trinity College Cambridge Medieval Manuscripts catalogue

dateStatements.label: production
dateStatements.notes: The PPEA introduction refines the hand to around the turn of the fifteenth century; this manifest preserves the holding catalog's broader object date.

entities.description: A fourteenth-century parchment codex whose first booklet preserves the B version of Piers Plowman at ff. 1r–130v; subsequent leaves contain a Treatise of Sin and a short poem.

holding.notes: The official catalog places Piers Plowman at ff. 1–130v and the Treatise of Sin at ff. 131–147r; the exact IIIF labels normalize the poem's opening as f. 1r.

entities.label: Trinity College Cambridge MS B.15.17

notes: The official catalog reports 28–30 lines per page and gives a detailed collation.
The work occupies almost the whole codex but not the later texts or binding surfaces.

rights.basis: The underlying work was composed in 1377–1379, centuries before modern copyright; the U.S. duration framework does not extend copyright to the medieval text itself.
rights.component: The underlying fourteenth-century Piers Plowman B-version text
notes: This statement covers neither Schmidt's modern editorial choices nor the U-M/OTA encoding, manuscript photography, or interface assets.

access.accessMethod: Official manuscript viewer and exact 156-canvas IIIF Presentation manifest
access.entityLabel: Trinity College Cambridge complete published IIIF facsimile of MS B.15.17
access.format: IIIF Presentation 2 and Image API JPEG derivatives

localCopies.label: Complete Trinity College Cambridge MS B.15.17 IIIF facsimile bundle
notes: Complete coverage of every canvas in the provider's 156-canvas Presentation manifest, including front and back covers and adjacent texts outside the work portion.
All 156 archived image members match the inventory byte counts and SHA-256 values; unzip integrity testing passed.
Every image used the non-upscaling IIIF request profile full/1600,/0/default.jpg. These are practical access derivatives, not preservation masters.
Representative inspection confirmed full-color photography at the front cover, f. 1r, the f. 130v–f. 131r boundary, and the back cover.

workPortion.label: Piers Plowman (B version)
locators: Trinity College Cambridge MS B.15.17, ff. 1r–130v
notes: Canvas 5 is the opening f. 1r text leaf.
Canvas 135 is a shared spread: Piers Plowman ends on f. 130v at left and the Treatise of Sin begins on f. 131r at right. The complete bundle deliberately retains both sides.
The ignored local cache is a private research copy and does not itself authorize redistribution beyond Trinity's item-level license.

rights.basis: The exact manuscript catalog and IIIF manifest identify the images as copyright the Master and Fellows of Trinity College Cambridge and license them under CC BY-NC 4.0.
rights.component: Trinity College Cambridge manuscript photographs and IIIF image files
rights.jurisdiction: Worldwide, subject to the item-level license
notes: The license applies to Trinity's images, not to Schmidt's critical edition or the physical manuscript as an object.
Attribution and noncommercial conditions remain relevant to any redistribution of the cached derivatives.

entities.description: The official 156-canvas color digital surrogate, including front and back covers and the manuscript sequence exposed by Trinity's Presentation manifest.
entities.label: Trinity College Cambridge complete published IIIF facsimile of MS B.15.17
notes: Complete means every canvas in Trinity's published 156-canvas surrogate was captured; it does not claim preservation-master resolution or every possible physical angle of the codex.

rights.basis: The exact manuscript catalog and IIIF manifest identify the images as copyright the Master and Fellows of Trinity College Cambridge and license them under CC BY-NC 4.0.
rights.component: Trinity College Cambridge manuscript photographs and IIIF image files
rights.jurisdiction: Worldwide, subject to the item-level license
notes: The license applies to Trinity's images, not to Schmidt's critical edition or the physical manuscript as an object.
Attribution and noncommercial conditions remain relevant to any redistribution of the cached derivatives.

entities.label: Piers Plowman Electronic Archive documentary edition of W

access.accessMethod: Internet Archive controlled digital lending item
access.entityLabel: Internet Archive controlled-access scan of Schmidt's 1978 edition
access.format: Restricted page images and encrypted lending files
notes: The metadata endpoint was public, but direct PDF and OCR requests returned HTTP 401. No restricted file was cached or treated as inspected.

entities.description: An Internet Archive scan record for the exact 1978 edition. Public metadata identifies the book, but PDF and OCR files were access-restricted and the scan contents were not inspected anonymously.
entities.label: Internet Archive controlled-access scan of Schmidt's 1978 edition
notes: The public metadata and restricted-file responses establish the access route, not page-level completeness or the exact copy's internal condition.

next_steps: Borrow or inspect a complete copy of Schmidt's exact 1978 edition, including the full introduction, manuscript list, apparatus, and lineation statement.
Record contributing witness entities and collated_against relations only when their sigla and passage scopes can be verified.
Compare the p. 188 running-title artifact, duplicate-key Latin continuations, and missing numbered positions against the printed pages before asserting a canonical verse-line total.
notes: The exact Internet Archive scans located were controlled-access items; no authentication or access control was bypassed.
openQuestions.question: Which witnesses supplied Schmidt's selected variant readings, how were they used, and how should the edition's intended lineation be reconciled with the XML's duplicate and missing N labels?

next_steps: Ask OTA and U-M for accession, production, transfer, and revision records for U-1687-A / PPlLan.
Ask Kyvero Press whether the CME import preserved the provider bytes or applied transformations before the recorded git blob.
Keep copied_from supported rather than assigning undocumented encoder agents or production dates.
openQuestions.question: Who keyed and encoded PPlLan, from which physical copy of Schmidt 1978, and what corrections or transformations occurred between the 1993 OTA/U-M text and the CME git artifact?

next_steps: Ask OTA why the advertised item-1687 bitstreams deny anonymous delivery and whether accession 3261 was withdrawn, renumbered, or can be restored.
Ask OTA and U-M for the original permission record covering Schmidt's 1978 edited text and for an item-specific statement on commercial redistribution.
Ask Kyvero Press whether the root Unlicense was intended to cover CME XML data and markup as well as software, without treating that answer as authority over third-party editorial rights.
openQuestions.question: What current item-level permission governs reuse of the PPlLan transcription and markup, and is OTA accession 3261 a recoverable replacement for item 1687?

scope.notes: The matching internal identifier, OTA accession, header, title, source statement, and text support the derivation; the exact import date into the CME git repository is undocumented.

reviewNotes: The B-version work, Trinity B.15.17, Schmidt's critical edition, the U-M/OTA encoding, and the General Corpus XML are distinct layers.
Schmidt's text is based on W but is not modeled as a diplomatic transcription because its title statement explicitly advertises selected variant readings.
The complete cached IIIF bundle covers the provider's published 156-canvas codex surrogate; the work portion ends on the left side of the shared f. 130v–f. 131r spread.
Current OTA catalog access, denied anonymous bitstreams, the dead replacement pointer, legacy personal-scholarly-use language, and the current OTA Academic Use terms are preserved separately.
The repository's root Unlicense speaks of software; its application to inherited XML data and markup is not broadened without file-specific evidence.

sourceLinks.accessMethod: Piers Plowman Electronic Archive W edition and manuscript introduction
sourceLinks.entityLabel: Piers Plowman Electronic Archive documentary edition of W

lineage.summary: The repository XML is a copy of a 1993 U-M/OTA electronic text derived from A. V. C. Schmidt's 1978 critical B-text. Schmidt explicitly based that edition on Trinity College Cambridge MS B.15.17 (W), while also supplying selected variant readings from other witnesses not yet identified passage by passage from a complete copy. A complete 156-canvas Trinity IIIF facsimile is publicly available and cached at a 1600-pixel-wide derivative profile; the poem occupies ff. 1r–130v, canvases 5–135, within the larger codex. The Piers Plowman Electronic Archive's documentary W edition is modeled as collateral scholarship, not as an upstream source of PPlLan.

catalogSummary.author: William Langland (attributed)
catalogSummary.region: Associated linguistically with the South West Midlands; exact place of composition unknown
catalogSummary.title: Piers Plowman (B version)

catalogingSubject.label: Piers Plowman (B version)
catalogingSubject.scope_note: The cataloged intellectual unit is the B version of the Middle English alliterative dream vision commonly called Piers Plowman. It includes its Prologue and twenty Passus, but excludes the distinct A and C versions, the broader Piers Plowman tradition, Trinity College Cambridge MS B.15.17 as a physical codex, A. V. C. Schmidt's 1978 critical edition and apparatus, the U-M/OTA electronic encoding, and the repository XML artifact.

contentParts.label: B-version poem: Prologue and Passus I–XX

locators.value: canvases 5–135 of 156; canvas 135 is the shared f. 130v–f. 131r spread

notes: The twenty-one-section summary follows the represented edition and XML hierarchy; it does not attempt to model every dream subdivision or every editorial line-number anomaly.
contentParts.summary: The complete B-version work represented in the encoding, from the Prologue's field full of folk through Conscience's final departure to seek Piers the Plowman.

assertion.notes: This is the composition range conventionally assigned to the B version, not the date of Trinity B.15.17, Schmidt's edition, or the electronic encoding.

extent.description: The extent models one verse work divided structurally into one Prologue and twenty Passus. The encoding has 7,598 nonempty L elements and 7,256 distinct section-plus-N lineation keys. There are 342 additional L elements sharing a key, chiefly marked Latin continuations; a running-title artifact, The Vision of Piers Plowman, is also encoded as an L at printed p. 188. Summing the terminal N values of the 21 sections gives 7,275 numbered positions, but 19 positions are absent. Because raw tags, distinct N keys, and numbered spans are not equivalent intellectual line counts, and the complete 1978 edition was not publicly inspectable, no canonical verse-line total is asserted.

formStatements.description: A long alliterative poem organized as a Prologue and twenty Passus, with embedded Latin quotations and occasional non-verse textual artifacts in the electronic line-tag stream.

languageStatements.label: Latin
notes: The XML contains hundreds of line elements with marked Latin content; many share a lineation number with the adjacent Middle English verse and must not be counted mechanically as additional canonical lines.
A brief French or Anglo-Norman inset also occurs, but it is retained as a note rather than promoted to a whole-work discovery language from one isolated quotation.

scope.description: Intermittent quotations, tags, and continuations embedded within the Middle English verse

notes: The preferred title and cataloging subject make the B-version boundary explicit; A and C are related versions, not unmodeled portions of this item.
Langland is retained as an attributed author. The wording does not claim that the conventional name is a securely documented biography.
Trinity B.15.17 is the principal base witness of Schmidt's critical text, but Schmidt's selected variants make the edition non-diplomatic.
The twenty-one-section and XML line-tag summaries are navigational descriptions, not claims that electronic element boundaries are medieval authorial or canonical line counts.
The complete cached codex contains the work at ff. 1r–130v and also preserves covers and the subsequent texts through f. 147; those adjacent materials are outside the cataloged work.

next_steps: Inspect a complete copy of Schmidt's exact 1978 edition, including its textual notes and lineation explanation.
Compare a sample of duplicate-key Latin continuations, missing N positions, and the p. 188 running-title artifact against the printed pages.
Keep verse_lines null until an intellectual count can be distinguished from XML tag and numbering counts.
openQuestions.question: How should the XML's 7,598 L elements, 7,256 distinct section-plus-N keys, 7,275 numbered-span positions, and omitted or duplicate labels be reconciled with Schmidt's intended lineation and a defensible canonical B-version line count?

next_steps: Consult Schmidt's complete introduction, list of manuscripts, and apparatus in the exact 1978 edition.
Do not model unnamed witnesses as direct source entities or infer passage ranges from the subtitle alone.
openQuestions.question: Which additional witnesses supply Schmidt's selected variant readings, and at which passages do those readings enter the 1978 text or apparatus?

assertion.notes: The evidence concerns the poem's linguistic and authorial associations; it does not establish the place of composition or the copying place of Trinity B.15.17.
This records the dominant scholarly attribution without converting an uncertain medieval author identity into a biographically established fact.

scope.description: Editorial responsibility for the 1978 source edition represented by the encoding, not responsibility for the medieval work
Selected variant readings in the 1978 critical edition; the contributing witness sigla and supplied ranges remain unverified from a complete copy

subjects.label: Social estates, law, exploitation, and institutional corruption

summaries.value: In a sequence of dreams, the wanderer Will searches for the meaning of Christian truth and for a way to live justly. Allegorical figures and social estates debate truth, labor, charity, confession, law, learning, poverty, clerical authority, and salvation; the poem's successive quests for Dowel, Dobet, and Dobest culminate in a vision of Christ, the founding and corruption of the Barn of Unity, and Conscience setting out again to seek Piers the Plowman.

The represented B version consists of a Prologue followed by Passus I–XX. The source XML maps this poem to Schmidt's printed pp. 1–263, while Trinity College Cambridge MS B.15.17 preserves its base-witness text at ff. 1r–130v; f. 131r begins a different codex item.

The source header calls the item The Vision of Piers Plowman and identifies Schmidt's 1978 critical B-text based on Trinity B.15.17 with selected variant readings. This record therefore catalogs the B-version work, while the manuscript, edition, encoding, and repository artifact remain separate lineage entities; it does not make claims about all A-, B-, and C-version readings.

assertion.notes: The parenthetical version label fixes the cataloging boundary and is not presented as a medieval title.

work.summary: In a sequence of dreams, the wanderer Will searches for the meaning of Christian truth and for a way to live justly. Allegorical figures and social estates debate truth, labor, charity, confession, law, learning, poverty, clerical authority, and salvation; the poem's successive quests for Dowel, Dobet, and Dobest culminate in a vision of Christ, the founding and corruption of the Barn of Unity, and Conscience setting out again to seek Piers the Plowman.
